species_description: An ancient species famous for its precious caviar and delicious meat, known for rapid growth and stress resistance.

A potamodromous sturgeon that completes its entire life cycle in freshwater. Due to its ecological adaptability, resilience, and shorter maturation time, it is an important model species for sturgeon conservation and aquaculture research. It is significant for the development of sturgeon aquaculture, as its production is very important for both meat and caviar.

It is a finfish, specifically the European eel, cultivated in intensive aquaculture setups like recirculated freshwater systems. At the 10 to 20 g stage (yellow eels), they are sexually undifferentiated. The purpose of this species is for commercial aquaculture, where it is prone to parasitic infestations requiring chemical treatments.
This is higle edible species but in this paper it is used as eel kidney cell line EK-1 for research purpose.

A species whose tadpoles are used in research to evaluate the acute toxicity and genotoxic potential of chemicals like formaldehyde, which is used in frog farming. The study aims to manage potential contamination and disease vectors associated with frog farming escapes into the environment.

A sea urchin species whose embryos are utilized in bioassays to evaluate the environmental toxicity of chemical compounds, such as antibiotics and disinfectants, associated with land-based marine fish farms.

A species of brine shrimp that is widely utilized in the aquaculture industry as a crucial live feed for the early larval stages of various marine fish and crustaceans. Due to its resilience and rapid reproduction, it is also frequently used as a standard model organism in ecotoxicology bioassays and scientific research.

A brine shrimp species whose cysts and freshly hatched nauplii are utilized as a model organism in acute toxicity bioassays. In this context, its purpose is to evaluate the toxicity and safety of essential oils being tested as potential antibacterial phyto-pharmaceuticals for the aquaculture industry.
Commonly known as the goldfish, it is one of the most important species among freshwater ornamental fish.

A snakehead fish that is the origin of the SSN-1 and E-11 authenticated cell lines. These cell cultures are used in scientific research to study the pathogenesis and interactions of fish viruses, such as the Tilapia tilapinevirus (TiLV).

A marine fish species that is widely cultured and consumed in Australia, Indonesia, and China. In aquaculture, it is vulnerable to diseases and inflammation caused by marine bacteria, which can result in economic losses for fishermen.

An Indian major carp and cultivable fish species widely used in aquaculture. In research, it is utilized as a bioindicator organism to assess the ecotoxicity and physiological impacts of environmental contaminants like antibiotics.
An indigenous African freshwater catfish that is also found in other tropical countries. It serves as an attractive model for ecotoxicological research due to its year-round availability.

The native fish species most commonly produced in South American continental aquaculture. Its commercial production frequently faces financial losses due to parasitic infections, specifically by the acanthocephalan Neoechinorhynchus buttnerae.

A freshwater fish species widely raised in aquaculture that is highly susceptible to white spot disease caused by the parasite Ichthyophthirius multifiliis. In this study, it is used as the host organism to evaluate the therapeutic efficacy and acute toxicity of emodin, an antiparasitic compound extracted from Chinese herbal medicine.

A freshwater, omnivorous fish found in lakes and rivers (specifically noted in Nepal) that feeds on aquatic plants and benthic worms. While its wild populations are considered in danger of extinction by the IUCN, its varieties have been domesticated and distributed worldwide for intensive aquaculture.

A fish species whose larvae and embryos are widely utilized as a model organism in ecotoxicological studies. In this context, their purpose is to help researchers assess the cardiovascular toxicity and potential adverse outcome pathways of environmental pollutants, such as the insecticide emamectin benzoate used in aquaculture.

A representative aquatic invertebrate considered a sensitive species and recommended by the U.S. EPA as a test organism for toxicity tests. In this context, it is used in a flow-through test system to assess the chronic toxicity and environmental risks of chemicals (like hydrogen peroxide) released from aquaculture facilities.

A marine fish species widely reared in intensive aquaculture systems. In this study, it serves as an experimental model to evaluate the single and combined genotoxic and cytotoxic impacts of common intensive aquaculture chemicals like formaldehyde and oxytetracycline.

An economically important marine aquaculture fish species widely cultured in China and Southeast Asian countries. Its commercial farming is heavily threatened by viral diseases, particularly the grouper iridovirus (GIV), which causes high mortality rates and severe financial losses to the industry.

An economically important hybrid fish species used in aquaculture. Research is conducted on this species to evaluate how dietary supplementation with Chinese herbal medicine (CHM) compounds can improve its growth performance, immune response, intestinal microbiota, and serum metabolism.

A marine fish species frequently found near Atlantic salmon (Salmo salar) farms. In this study, it serves as a non-target species to evaluate the environmental impacts, uptake kinetics, and toxicological effects of diflubenzuron, a drug used to treat salmon lice in marine aquaculture.

A widely disseminated freshwater fish species native to the United States. In this context, it serves as an experimental test organism to assess the acute ecotoxicological effects such as oxidative stress, neurotoxicity, and histological changes caused by exposure to the antibiotic tetracycline in aquatic environments.

A species of coral that is highly susceptible to ciliate infections, which cause pathological changes such as tissue bleaching, ulceration, and the production of a jelly-like substance (brown jelly syndrome). Effective treatments (like clove extract) are being researched to curb the rapid spread and high mortality of these infections within the global aquarium trade.

A benthic non-target arthropod that represents one of the most valuable commercial fisheries in Canada. While landings have reached all-time highs in many areas, some regions (such as LFA 25 and 26A) have shown a decline in the abundance of 1-year-old lobsters, raising concerns about future landings. Beyond its commercial importance, it is also used as a model species in scientific research to study the metabolic and developmental effects of chronic exposure to environmental contaminants.

species_name: Hybrid grouper (Mycteroperca tigris x Epinephelus lanceolatus)
species_description: A hybrid fish species cultured in saltwater net-pens that is susceptible to sea lice (Copepoda: Caligidae) infestations, which negatively impact marine aquaculture. This species is used in research to test the efficacy of the pharmaceutical drug emamectin benzoate (EB) for controlling sea lice, as limited licensed treatments exist for tropical and semi-tropical farmed fish.

species_name: Hybrid striped bass (Morone chrysops × Morone saxatilis)
species_description: An economically important finfish species used in research to evaluate the growth-promoting effects of dietary oxytetracycline. The study concluded that oral administration of this antibiotic provided no growth-promoting benefits and did not significantly affect survival rates, suggesting no incentive for nontherapeutic use in U.S. aquaculture.

An economically important fish species in aquaculture that is susceptible to infections by the zoonotic pathogen Streptococcus iniae. It is used in research to evaluate the protective efficacy of new, environmentally friendly antibacterial agents (like thymol) against drug-resistant bacterial strains.

A freshwater Indian major carp that is widely cultivated in India due to its economic importance and ability to survive well in limited culture areas. It is a primary species contributing to freshwater fish production, though it is highly susceptible to the opportunistic pathogen Aeromonas hydrophila, which can lead to high mortality rates.

This species is the most productive mariculture fish in China, with an expanding aquaculture scale along the southeastern coast. However, its development has led to environmental concerns, particularly regarding the increased use of antibiotics and other chemicals.

An economically important aquaculture species often cultured in cage systems. This species is susceptible to natural infestations by the anchor worm Lernaea sp., a crustacean bloodsucker that causes high mortality rates and significant production losses.

A freshwater fish species susceptible to infections by digenean trematodes, which have complex life cycles. It is used as a model organism in research to evaluate the efficacy of injectable praziquantel for the elimination of trematode metacercariae.

A crustacean species often used in research to evaluate the effects of dietary supplements (such as astaxanthin) on growth and resistance to physical and chemical stress. It is a significant species in aquaculture, and scientific techniques are being applied to improve its production processes.

An important species in aquaculture that is susceptible to various bacterial pathogens, such as Edwardsiella spp., Vibrio spp., Aeromonas spp., Escherichia coli, and Salmonella spp.. It is used in research as a source of bacterial isolates to test the efficacy of alternative, environmentally friendly treatments like Cymbopogon nardus (citronella) essential oil against antibiotic-resistant bacteria.

An essential engineer species and shellfish reef builder that plays a vital role in coastal ecosystems. As a filter-feeder, it has been introduced worldwide and is currently one of the main aquaculture species globally, though it is sensitive to environmental stressors like climate change, pollutants, and pathogens.

An economically important fish species in aquaculture. It is used in research to evaluate the effectiveness of immunostimulants, such as the combination of β-glucan and Astragalus polysaccharide, against bacterial infections like Nocardia seriolae.
This is highly edible species. In this study it served as a source of Edwardsiella tarda for scientific research study

An economically important fish species in aquaculture. Research is conducted on this species to understand the mechanisms of chronic toxicity (such as oxidative stress and metabolic disorders) induced by pollutants like niclosamide, a commonly used molluscicide, which poses environmental risks.

A fish species whose early life stages (specifically seawater-adapted fry) are at risk of exposure to aquaculture chemotherapeutants used to control sea lice. In this research, it is used as a model organism to determine both lethal (LC50) and sublethal effects (such as altered olfactory responses and behavioral avoidance) caused by exposure to five specific chemotherapeutants: hydrogen peroxide, azamethiphos, emamectin benzoate, cypermethrin, and deltamethrin.

An economically important aquaculture species, ranking as the second most important globally. It is used as a model organism to evaluate the mitigation of dietary microplastic (MP) accumulation and oxidative stress through the supplementation of natural microencapsulated antioxidants like astaxanthin.
Cell line of this fish is mentioned in this paper and this fish is highly edible.

A significant, globally farmed tropical fish species known for its strong immune system, rapid growth rate, and high tolerance to diverse environmental conditions. It serves as a model species in aquaculture research to evaluate the biochemical and oxidative stress effects of aquatic pollutants, such as copper sulphate and copper oxide nanoparticles.

An important warm-water aquaculture species, cultured globally (including the U.S., Taiwan, and Latin America). It serves as a model organism in studies investigating emergent diseases in aquaculture, specifically piscine francisellosis caused by Francisella asiatica. Research on this species evaluates the efficacy of antibiotic treatments, such as florfenicol, to control these infections.

An important aquaculture species where researchers are investigating the use of the edible herb Bidens pilosa as a functional feed additive to reduce reliance on antibiotics and chemicals. Dietary supplementation (0.5% and 1%) of B. pilosa was found to significantly increase growth performance over 8 weeks, with transcriptomic analysis revealing regulation of genes and pathways involved in amino acid metabolism.

A crustacean species inhabiting the littoral zone that is susceptible to the negative environmental impacts of antiparasitic drugs, such as teflubenzuron, used to control sea lice in salmon aquaculture. It serves as a model species in research to examine molecular and transcriptional responses to sublethal chemical exposure, particularly to determine if non-hepatopancreatic tissue can be used to assess chemical stress.

A keystone species in the marine ecosystem and a valuable commercial crustacean. It is known to have high sensitivity to anti-parasitic chemicals (specifically azamethiphos and deltamethrin) used in salmon farming. In this study, it serves as a model organism to evaluate the effects of these aquaculture pesticides, alone and in combination, and to determine the influence of natural stressors like food limitation on shrimp larvae.

A shrimp species used in research to evaluate the accumulation, oxidative stress, and molecular (transcriptomic and metabolomic) responses to exposure to the antiparasitic insecticide teflubenzuron, which is used in salmon aquaculture. The study also assesses whether homogenized whole-shrimp specimens are suitable for examining the mechanistic effects of chemical stressors. This is also highly edible species.

A fish species often used in aquaculture research to study water quality management, particularly the removal of ammonia in culture systems. In this study, it serves as a model organism to evaluate the efficiency of natural and chemically modified stilbite (a zeolite mineral) for ammonia removal under controlled laboratory conditions.

A species utilized as a model organism in marine toxicology to monitor the toxicity of effluents from land-based marine fish farms. It is used in sea urchin embryo bioassays to evaluate the developmental effects of various chemicals associated with aquaculture, including antibiotics (like amoxicillin, flumequine, oxytetracycline) and disinfectants (like sodium hypochlorite and formaldehyde). It is highly edible and considered a gastronomic delicacy.

The most popular and economically significant aquaculture species in Korea. It is severely impacted by viral hemorrhagic septicemia virus (VHSV), which causes high mortality rates and constitutes 12.5% of olive flounder mortalities in Korea. It serves as a model species in experimental research to evaluate the antiviral effects of compounds like umbelliferone (7-hydroxycoumarin) as potential drug candidates for disease prevention.

An economically important aquaculture species. Its larviculture is often threatened by luminescence diseases caused by pathogenic Vibrio harveyi bacteria. It is used as a model species in research to test the efficacy of natural extracts (such as Ulva fasciata) in reducing bacterial growth and virulence factors to enhance shrimp survival.

An economically vital species for the global aquaculture market, accounting for approximately US$20 billion. Its farming is heavily threatened by fatal infectious diseases, including white spot disease, hepatopancreatic microsporidiosis, and acute hepatopancreatic necrosis disease (AHPND), leading to major economic losses. It is widely used in experimental research to test therapeutic agents against pathogenic strains of Vibrio bacteria.
A species of bivalve used in aquaculture. Research is conducted on this species to assess the potential negative physiological effects of candidate ascidian antifoulant compounds.

The second most produced native fish species in Brazil. It serves as a model species in aquaculture research to evaluate the efficacy of therapeutic agents, such as teflubenzuron, for treating ectoparasitic infections like Trichodina spp.. Additionally, its acute toxicity levels to these therapeutic agents are assessed to ensure safety in intensive farming environments.
Primarily used as bait or forage fish. In this study it's cell line is udes for research.

A popular ornamental fish species often used in research as a model system to test the efficacy of commercial products and essential oils against ectoparasitic helminths like Gyrodactylus turnbulli. It is a vital species in the hobbyist trade, and studies on it help identify effective treatments for parasitic infections that cause economic damage in fish populations.

Commonly known as the narrow-clawed crayfish, this species plays an essential role in biomonitoring ambient water quality through its biochemical, behavioral, and physiological reactions. In this context, its purpose is to serve as an experimental bioindicator to assess cardiac stress responses (heart rate changes) to varying concentrations of chloramine-T, a biocide used in industry and aquaculture.

A main host of the white spot syndrome virus (WSSV) and serves as an important model organism for researching and developing antiviral agents to combat white spot disease (WSD) in crustacean aquaculture. Its susceptibility to WSSV makes it ideal for testing the antiviral efficiency of common drugs like acyclovir and exploring pharmacological mechanisms of immune response.

A hybrid fish species commonly used in aquaculture. In this study, it serves as an experimental model to investigate the effects of malachite green (MG) and its metabolite leuco-MG (LMG) residues, focusing on biochemical profiles, oxidative stress, and residue accumulation under tropical conditions.

The most cultivated native fish species in Rio Grande do Sul, southern Brazil, known for its ease of handling, fast growth, and tolerance to cold. It serves as a model species in aquaculture research to evaluate the in vivo efficacy of natural extracts (such as Hesperozygis ringens leaf extract) for controlling bacterial infections, specifically Aeromonas hydrophila.

A significant species in aquaculture that is frequently exposed to hydrogen peroxide treatments to manage topical skin and gill infections, such as those caused by sea lice (Lepeophtheirus salmonis) and amoebic gill disease (Neoparamoeba perurans). It serves as a model in studies investigating how daily rhythms (circadian variations) influence the absorption, metabolism, and excretion of therapeutic substances, which is critical for optimizing drug efficacy and minimizing toxicity.

A migrating salmonid fish that is economically important in Poland, particularly following the extinction of Atlantic salmon in the 1980s. It serves as a model organism in research to assess how environmental pollutants, such as varying concentrations of selenium exposure during egg swelling, impact the survival rates of its embryos and fry.

A prevalent soft coral species found in Sanya, Hainan province, China. It serves as a model species in research investigating the impact of contamination by doxycycline hydrochloride (DOX)—an aquatic drug—on coral health by measuring physiological, biochemical, and symbiotic indicators.

An aquatic animal species used in the study as a source of bacterial isolates (Vibrio spp.). Researchers utilized these isolates to evaluate the potential of C. nardus (citronella) essential oil as an alternative to commercial antibiotics for treating bacterial infections in aquaculture.

A commercially important species in aquaculture that frequently faces significant challenges due to parasitic infections, such as Microcotyle sebastis. It serves as a model organism in research to evaluate the efficacy and safety of alternative anthelmintic treatments, like levamisole hydrochloride, to combat resistant parasites and optimize disease management in aquaculture.

An important species in aquaculture that suffers from parasitic infections, such as those caused by monogeneans. Research on this species focuses on developing effective, taste-masked delivery methods for antiparasitic medications like praziquantel (PZQ) to improve treatment compliance, as fish often avoid medicated feed due to the drug's unpalatability.

A teleost fish species widely used in aquaculture and frequently employed as a model organism in studies investigating the immunostimulatory effects of medicinal plants. Research on this species explores alternative, environmentally friendly prophylactic methods—such as the use of dihydroquercetin (from Cedrus deodara)—to enhance the fish immune system and prevent/treat diseases as an alternative to chemicals.

These fish are grown in aquaculture and are susceptible to bacterial pathologies and mechanical injuries. They were used in a study to evaluate the vulnerary (wound-healing) effect of botanical medicines specifically herbal infusions of Chelidonium majus L., Hypericum perforatum L., and Bidens tripartita L. to treat bacterial infections in a controlled environment.

An aquatic animal species used as a source for Edwardsiella tarda bacterial isolates. These isolates are utilized in research to evaluate the antimicrobial efficacy of natural treatments, such as Cymbopogon nardus (citronella) essential oil, as an alternative to commercial antibiotics in aquaculture.
Highly valued, edible marine fish with major commercial importance. In this study it is obtained from marine cages from local farms for research.

An aquatic species that acts as a source for Edwardsiella tarda bacterial isolates. In research, these isolates are used to evaluate the antibacterial efficacy of natural treatments, such as Cymbopogon nardus (citronella) essential oil, to combat potential fish pathogens in aquaculture.
